title: Distillation Tradeoffs
concept: Distilled models are highly efficient for narrow tasks, but lose the broad, general reasoning capabilities of the larger teacher model. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Fine-Tuning & Adaptation.'

options.text: Making a model larger and slower
Shrinking a model by training it to mimic a larger model
Connecting a model directly to the internet

exercises.explain: Distillation is the process of transferring knowledge from a large, complex model to a smaller, faster one.

cards.title: The Cost of Speed
cards.body: We know that shrinking a giant AI model makes it faster and cheaper. But does this superpower come with a catch? Let's find out what we have to give up when we make AI models smaller!

cards.title: What is the Distillation Tradeoff?
cards.body: Model distillation copies knowledge from a giant 'teacher' AI into a smaller 'student' AI. While the student becomes incredibly fast and cheap to run, it loses the broad, general-purpose reasoning skills of the teacher. It becomes a specialist, not a generalist!

cards.title: The Pocket Guide vs. The Library
cards.body: Think of the giant teacher model as a massive, multi-floor university library containing books on every topic imaginable. A distilled model is like a tiny, specialized pocket guide written specifically for a single tour. It tells you exactly where to go on your day trip, but it can't help you write a philosophy thesis or learn quantum physics!

cards.title: Coding Specialist
cards.body: Suppose you distill a giant AI to only write Python code. The new student model will write Python amazingly fast and use very little computer memory. However, if you suddenly ask it to write a poem about space or translate French, it will struggle or fail completely, whereas the giant teacher model could do both easily.

cards.title: Myth: Shrinking Has No Downsides
cards.body: People often think a distilled model is just as smart as the original teacher model in every single way. In reality, while the student model matches the teacher on the specific task it was trained for, it loses the teacher's broad reasoning and common-sense logic on other tasks.

cards.title: Teacher vs. Student
cards.body: Let's compare the capabilities of a giant teacher model versus its smaller, distilled student model.

left.text: High accuracy across math, coding, poetry, and philosophy. Very slow and expensive to run.

right.text: Extremely fast and cheap, but only works well on one specific task (like sorting emails).

exercises.prompt: Which of the following best describes the tradeoff of using a distilled model?

options.text: It makes the model much slower and larger.
It is highly efficient for narrow tasks but loses general reasoning.
It allows the model to learn new languages instantly.
It increases the memory usage of the model on mobile phones.

exercises.explain: Distillation reduces the size and cost of a model, making it highly efficient for specific tasks, but it loses broad, general-purpose intelligence.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A distilled student model retains 100% of the teacher model's general reasoning capabilities across all topics.
exercises.explain: Distilled models lose general reasoning capabilities and are best suited for specific, narrow applications.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe the focus of a distilled student model.
exercises.sentence: Distilled models are great for a [blank] task, but they lose general reasoning.
exercises.explain: Distilled models are highly optimized for narrow, specific tasks, but they lose the broad general reasoning of the teacher model.

exercises.prompt: Which of these AIs is showing the typical behavior of a distilled student model?

options.text: AI 1: It answers math questions, writes stories, and translates Spanish with high accuracy.
AI 2: It filters spam emails incredibly fast, but fails completely when asked to write a creative story.
AI 3: It takes ten minutes to answer any simple question and frequently crashes.

exercises.explain: AI 2 is the distilled model because it is highly optimized and fast for its specific task (filtering emails), but fails at a general task (creative writing). AI 1 is the general teacher model, and AI 3 is just broken.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you need to deploy a customer service bot to a cheap smartphone. Why would you choose a distilled model over the giant teacher model, and what is the main trade-off?

options.text: It runs much faster on cheap hardware but loses general reasoning.
It becomes larger and more expensive to run than the teacher.
It keeps all general knowledge but runs much slower.
It requires more memory but gets better at writing poetry.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Distillation creates a fast, lean model for specific tasks, but it forgets how to handle general, unrelated reasoning. Next up: Fine-Tuning & Adaptation.
